questions.question: The reviewer starts with the symbol of a city map in order to illustrate the
questions.passage:
James Baxter reviews two books about the internet: Rewire by Ethan Zuckerman, and Untangling the Web by Aleks Krotoski. Open a street map of any city and you see a diagram of all the possible routes one could take in traversing or exploring it. Superimpose on the street map the actual traffic flows that are observed and you see quite a different city: one of flows. The flows show how people actually travel in the city, as distinct from how they could. This helps in thinking about the internet and digital technology generally. In itself, the technology has vast possibilities, as several recent books emphasize, but what we actually wind up doing with it is, at any point in time, largely unknown.

Ethan Zuckerman is excited by the possibilities the web provides for linking far-flung populations, for sampling different ways of life, for making us all digital cosmopolitans. His central thesis, however, is that while the internet does, in principle, enable everyone to become genuinely cosmopolitan, in practice it does nothing of the kind. As the philosopher Anthony Appiah puts it, true cosmopolitanism 'challenges us to embrace what is rich, productive and creative' about differences; in other words, to go beyond merely being tolerant of those who are different. Much of the early part of Rewire is taken up with demonstrating the extent to which the internet, and our use of it, fails that test.

'We shape our tools,' said the philosopher Marshall McLuhan, 'and afterwards they shape us.' This adage is corroborated every time most of us go online. We've built information tools (like search and social networking systems) that embody our biases towards things that affect those who are closest to us. They give us the information we think we want, but not necessarily the information we might need.

Despite all the connectivity, we are probably as ignorant about other societies as we were when television and newspapers were our main information sources. In fact, Zuckerman argues, in some ways we were better then, because serious mainstream media outlets saw it as their professional duty to 'curate' the flow of news; there were editorial gatekeepers who determined a 'news agenda' of what was and wasn't important. But, as the internet went mainstream, we switched from curation to search, and the traditional gatekeepers became less powerful. In some respects, this was good because it weakened large multimedia conglomerates, but it had the unanticipated consequence of increasing the power of digital search tools - and, indirectly, the power of the corporations providing them.

Zuckerman - a true cosmopolitan who co-founded a web service dedicated to realizing the net's capacity to enable anyone's voice to be heard - provides an instructive contrast to excessively optimistic narratives about the transformative power of networked technology, and a powerful diagnosis of what's wrong. Where he runs out of steam somewhat is in contemplating possible solutions, of which he identifies three: 'transparent translation' - simply automated, accurate translation between all languages; 'bridge figures' - bloggers who explain ideas from one culture to another; and 'engineered serendipity' - basically, technology for enabling us to escape from filters that limit search and networking systems. Eventually, the technology will deliver transparent translation; cloning Ethan Zuckerman would provide a supply of bridge figures, but, for now, we will have to make do with pale imitations. Engineering serendipity, however, is a tougher proposition.

Aleks Krotoski might be able to help. She is a keen observer of our information ecosystem, and has been doing the conference rounds with an intriguing contraption called the 'Serendipity Engine', which is two parts art installation and one part teaching tool. Untangling the Web is a collection of 17 thoughtful essays on the impact of comprehensive networking on our lives. They cover the spectrum of stuff we need to think about - from the obvious (like privacy, identity and the social impact of the net) to topics which don't receive enough attention (for example, what medics, with a sniff, call 'cyberchondria' - how the net can increase health anxieties).

Although she's a glamorous media 'star' (having fronted a TV series about the internet), people underestimate Krotoski at their peril. She's a rare combination of academic, geek, reporter and essayist, which her chapter on the concept of friendship online exemplifies: she's read what the key social theorists say on the subject, but she's also alert to what she experiences as 'emotional anemia' - 'the sense that....you might not feel the online love from the people you should, because your nearest and dearest may be drowned out in the ocean of sociability.' Which, in a way, brings us back to Zuckerman's thoughts about the difference between what networked technology could do and what it actually does.
options: difficulty in understanding the complexity of the internet.
degree to which the internet changes as time passes.
difference between potential and real internet use.
importance of the internet in people's lives today.
questions.explanation: The map analogy shows possible routes (potential) vs actual traffic flows (real). This illustrates the difference between what the internet could do and what people actually do with it.

questions.question: What do the words 'that test' in line 13 refer to?
options: providing more widespread access to information
connecting in a substantial way with other cultures
establishing principles for developing the internet
accepting that not everyone in the world is the same
questions.explanation: The test refers to true cosmopolitanism: embracing differences, going beyond tolerance. That means connecting substantially with other cultures.

questions.question: What point is made about the internet in the third paragraph?
options: People often struggle to find what they are looking for on it.
It influences how people relate to family and friends.
All users have some responsibility for its evolution.
The way in which it works is far from neutral.
questions.explanation: The third paragraph says information tools embody biases, give us what we think we want but not what we need. This shows the internet is not neutral; it works with built-in biases.

questions.question: What does the reviewer suggest about Zuckerman in the fifth paragraph?
options: His recommendations are less impressive than his analysis.
He uses terms that are harder to understand than need be.
He has the same failings that he identifies in other people.
His account of important developments is too negative.
questions.explanation: The paragraph says 'Where he runs out of steam somewhat is in contemplating possible solutions' — his analysis/diagnosis is strong, but his recommendations are less impressive.

questions.question: Which of the following words is used to suggest disapproval? (paragraph 6)
questions.explanation: 'Contraption' often implies a strange or awkward device, suggesting disapproval or amusement. The other words are neutral or not disapproving.

questions.question: What does the reviewer suggest about Aleks Krotoski in the final paragraph?
options: Her insight into the nature of online friendship is perceptive.
She has been influenced by Ethan Zuckerman.
People are often misled by her academic credentials.
She takes on too many different roles.
questions.explanation: The reviewer praises her chapter on online friendship as exemplary, showing she is alert to 'emotional anemia' — so her insight is perceptive.

questions.question: The word 'adage' paragraph 3 means
questions.explanation: Adage means a proverb or wise saying.

questions.question: The phrase 'far-flung' paragraph 2 refers to
questions.explanation: Far-flung means distant or remote.

questions.question: Which one of the following is a possible title for the passage?
questions.explanation: The passage is a review of two books, but primarily focuses on 'Rewire' by Ethan Zuckerman. 'Rewire' is mentioned as the first book and the reviewer discusses its thesis extensively.

questions.question: Which of the following is a technology for enabling us to escape from filters that limit search and networking systems?
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly defines 'engineered serendipity' as technology for escaping filters that limit search and networking systems.

questions.question: The sudden demise of the old political regime took the international community by surprise.
options: The sudden rebirth of the old political regime...
The sudden collapse and death of the old political regime...
The sudden strengthening of the old political regime...
The sudden compromise of the old political regime...
questions.explanation: Demise means death or collapse. Option B 'collapse and death' captures the meaning. Rebirth and strengthening are opposites, compromise is not the same.

questions.question: The artist's unconventional methods, using industrial waste as a medium, challenged the art world.
options: The artist's unorthodox and non-traditional methods...
questions.explanation: Unconventional means not traditional, unorthodox. Option B 'unorthodox and non-traditional' is the best synonym. Haphazard means random, not the same.

questions.question: Without innovation, the company's growth began to stagnate, leading to an investor's anxiety.
options: ...began to become stagnant and stop developing...
questions.explanation: Stagnate means to stop developing or become inactive. 'Become stagnant and stop developing' matches. Accelerate is opposite, fluctuate means vary.

questions.question: The synergy between the marketing and design departments resulted in a highly successful campaign.
options: The combined power that produces a greater effect between the departments...
questions.explanation: Synergy means combined action producing a greater effect than the sum of individual efforts. Option B captures this.

questions.question: The negotiation was a compromise; neither side got everything they wanted, but both could accept the deal.
options: The negotiation was a total victory...
The negotiation was a complete failure...
The negotiation was a settlement of differences by mutual concession...
The negotiation was a hostile takeover...
questions.explanation: Compromise means a settlement by mutual concession. Option C defines it accurately.

questions.question: The company is seizing the opportunity to expand into emerging markets before its competitors.
options: The company is ignoring the opportunity...
The company is evaluating the opportunity...
The company is quickly and decisively taking advantage of the opportunity...
The company is compromising on the opportunity...
questions.explanation: Seizing an opportunity means taking advantage of it quickly and decisively. Option C is correct.

questions.question: His chronic procrastination meant he was always working against tight deadlines.
options: His chronic hesitation and delay meant...
questions.explanation: Procrastination means delaying or postponing tasks. 'Hesitation and delay' is the best synonym. Efficiency and diligence are opposites.

questions.question: The ongoing investigations into the affair have yet to yield any conclusive evidence.
options: The ongoing formal inquiries and examinations have yet to yield any conclusive evidence.
The ongoing celebrations have yet to yield any conclusive evidence.
The ongoing negotiations have yet to yield any conclusive evidence.
The ongoing prototypes have yet to yield any conclusive evidence.
questions.explanation: Investigations mean formal inquiries or examinations. Option A correctly substitutes.

questions.question: We must prioritize the client's most critical needs before addressing minor requests.
options: We must ignore the client's most critical needs...
We must designate as less important the client's most critical needs...
We must treat as of highest importance the client's most critical needs...
We must compromise on the client's most critical needs...
questions.explanation: Prioritize means treat as most important. Option C expresses that correctly.

questions.question: Climate change is a cross-cutting issue that affects, and is affected by, every sector of the economy.
options: ...a pervasive issue that affects many areas...
questions.explanation: Cross-cutting means affecting many different areas. 'Pervasive issue that affects many areas' matches.

questions.question: The philosopher's essay was a critique of modern _____, arguing that the relentless pursuit of possessions empties life of deeper meaning.
questions.explanation: Consumerism is the pursuit of material possessions. The sentence describes that pursuit, so consumerism is correct.

questions.question: Despite the farmer's careful use of _____, a resilient strain of beetles began to devastate the crop.
questions.explanation: Farmers use pesticides to kill insects. A resilient strain of beetles would survive pesticides, devastating the crop.

questions.question: Through the thick morning fog, the lookout on the ship could just barely _____ the outline of the distant coastline.
questions.explanation: Descry means to catch sight of or make out something distant or faint. In fog, the lookout could descry the coastline.

questions.question: The alarming data on plastic pollution in the oceans has led many scientists to _____ an immediate and global reduction in single-use plastics.
questions.explanation: Call for means to demand or request. Scientists would call for a reduction in plastics.

questions.question: The historian argued that the treaty's punitive conditions would _____ economic instability and, ultimately, a second conflict.
questions.explanation: Result in means to cause or lead to. Punitive conditions would result in instability and conflict.

questions.question: The exhibition of fragile ancient artefacts was carefully arranged to ensure that no visitor would accidentally _____ a priceless vase.
questions.explanation: Knock over means to cause to fall by bumping. Visitors might accidentally knock over a vase.

questions.question: Signs around the protected breeding ground clearly read, 'Please _____ the grass' to allow the endangered birds to nest undisturbed.
questions.explanation: Keep off means to stay away from. Signs saying 'keep off the grass' are common to protect the area.

questions.question: The _____ smoking area was placed well away from the building's entrances and air intake vents.
questions.explanation: Designated means officially assigned. A designated smoking area is set aside for that purpose.

questions.question: The critic dismissed the artist's latest work as _____ to her earlier masterpieces, lacking both the technical skill and emotional depth.
questions.explanation: Inferior means lower in quality. The new work is inferior to the earlier masterpieces.

questions.question: The review of the new play was unexpectedly _____, describing the performance as a 'brutal dismantling of theatrical convention.'
questions.explanation: Savage means extremely harsh or brutal. The review described a brutal dismantling, so savage fits.

questions.question: The election results in the _____ district were subjected to a recount after allegations of widespread irregularities.
questions.explanation: Contested means disputed or challenged. A contested district would have allegations and a recount.

questions.question: Arrange sentences: a. more that the scale of AIDS pandemic. b. scientists, economists and politicians made a powerful plea for international action to fight COVID 19. c. and demanded that the diseases be recognized as a major problem. d. they describe it as 'a disaster looming over the world'. e. so more effort should be exerted to overcome the virus.
questions.explanation: Logical order: b (scientists made plea) → d (they describe it as disaster) → c (demanded recognition) → a (more that the scale of AIDS) → e (so more effort). That is b,d,c,a,e which is option B.

questions.question: Arrange sentences: a. all these are very bad. b. it causes lung cancer. c. smoking is very dangerous habit. d. it also causes throat infection. e. it is therefore, good to avoid smoking altogether.
questions.explanation: Logical order: c (smoking dangerous) → b (causes lung cancer) → d (also causes throat infection) → a (all these bad) → e (therefore avoid). That is c,b,d,a,e which is option C.

questions.question: Arrange sentences: a. he dislike the idea of always being available. b. they want him to keep it on all the time. c. Alemu's company has bought him a mobile phone. d. he also hates the way some people talk loudly on the mobile. e. he usually is impatient with them.
questions.explanation: Logical order: c (company bought phone) → b (they want him always on) → a (he dislikes being always available) → d (also hates loud talkers) → e (impatient with them). That is c,b,a,d,e which is option A.

questions.question: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
options: She quickly turned down the offer without thinking.
She turned down quickly the offer without thinking.
he turned quickly down the offer without thinking.
She turned the offer down quickly without thinking.
questions.explanation: Option A has correct adverb placement ('quickly' before the phrasal verb 'turned down'). Option D is also acceptable but 'turned the offer down' is fine; however A is more standard. Among the choices, A is correct.

questions.question: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
options: Not only the project he completed on time, but also under budget.
Not only did he complete the project on time, but also under budget.
Not only he completed the project on time, but also under budget.
Not only completed he the project on time, but also under budget.
questions.explanation: Inversion with 'Not only' requires auxiliary verb before subject: 'Not only did he complete'.

questions.question: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
options: It was an Italian beautiful large ancient marble statue.
It was a beautiful large ancient Italian marble statue.
It was a marble ancient Italian large beautiful statue.
It was a large beautiful ancient marble Italian statue.
questions.explanation: Adjective order: opinion (beautiful) → size (large) → age (ancient) → origin (Italian) → material (marble). Option B follows this correctly.

questions.question: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
options: The proposal has been repeatedly rejected by the committee.
The proposal has been rejected repeatedly by the committee.
The proposal repeatedly has been rejected by the committee.
The proposal has repeatedly been rejected by the committee.
questions.explanation: All are grammatically possible, but the most natural placement of 'repeatedly' is after the first auxiliary or at the end. Option A 'has been repeatedly rejected' is standard. Option D 'has repeatedly been rejected' is also fine. However A is commonly used. Given typical test patterns, A is correct.

questions.question: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
options: Running late for the meeting, the train was missed by James.
Running late for the meeting, James missed the train.
Running late for the meeting, the train left without James.
Running late for the meeting, there was no train for James.
questions.explanation: The participial phrase 'Running late for the meeting' must modify the subject of the main clause. James is the one running late, so 'James missed the train' is correct. The other options have dangling modifiers.

questions.question: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
options: So compelling was his argument that the jury acquitted him immediately.
So compelling his argument was that the jury acquitted him immediately.
His argument was so compelling that the jury acquitted him immediately.
Both a and c are correct.
questions.explanation: Both A (inversion) and C (standard order) are grammatically correct. So option D is correct.

questions.question: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
options: Only she saw the critical error in the code.
She only saw the critical error in the code.
She saw only the critical error in the code.
She saw the only critical error in the code.
questions.explanation: Placement of 'only' changes meaning. 'Only she saw' means no one else saw it. The other options imply different meanings. Without context, A is a valid correct sentence. But all are grammatically correct. Typically, the test expects A as the most straightforward.

questions.question: If I had had free time, I ... shopping.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: If I had had free time, I would have gone shopping.

questions.question: If I were you, I ... that job.
questions.explanation: Present unreal conditional: If I were you, I would apply for that job.

questions.question: His manager was so angry because of ... late.
questions.explanation: After 'because of' we need a noun phrase. 'His being late' is a gerund phrase acting as a noun. 'He was' would be a clause, needing 'because' not 'because of'.

questions.question: Would you mind showing me ...?
options: how can I go to the post-office
questions.explanation: The polite request 'Would you mind showing me the way to the post-office?' is correct. The other options have incorrect word order for indirect questions.

questions.question: How long have you known her? ...
questions.explanation: Duration uses 'for' + period of time. 'For five years' is correct. 'Since' requires a point in time.

questions.question: My uncle has just arrived ...... Berlin.
questions.explanation: Arrive in + city/country. 'Arrived in Berlin' is correct.

questions.question: It ... a long time since she last saw me.
questions.explanation: It has been + time + since + past simple. 'It has been a long time since she last saw me.'

questions.question: I think she will join us, ...?
questions.explanation: Tag question for positive statement with 'will' is 'won't she'. 'I think she will join us, won't she?'

questions.question: Could you hang ...... a minute? I'll be right back.
questions.explanation: Phrasal verb 'hang on' means wait a short time. 'Hang on a minute' is correct.

questions.question: The man fell from the tree and ... consciousness.
questions.explanation: Lost consciousness means became unconscious. 'Lost' is correct.

questions.question: Your money will be refunded if the goods are not to your ......
questions.explanation: To your satisfaction is a fixed phrase meaning meeting your expectations. 'Satisfaction' is the correct noun.

questions.question: If he ...... harder, the results would be better.
questions.explanation: Present unreal conditional: If he worked harder (past subjunctive), the results would be better.

questions.question: Mary ...... to take her neighbours to court if they didn't stop making so much noise.
questions.explanation: Threatened to take them to court expresses an intention to cause harm if they didn't stop. Promised would be positive, suggested is not appropriate.

questions.question: In some schools, the training quality is worse than ...... used to be.
questions.explanation: The pronoun 'it' refers to 'the training quality'. 'It used to be' is correct.

questions.question: I was ... about to ring up his office when Mike arrived home.
questions.explanation: Just about to means almost ready to do something. 'I was just about to ring' is correct.

questions.question: I'll be disappointed if you ... the exam.
questions.explanation: First conditional: 'if you don't pass' (present simple in condition clause). 'Won't pass' is incorrect in condition clause.

questions.question: Could you ... me some money?
questions.explanation: Lend means give temporarily expecting return. 'Could you lend me some money?' is correct. Borrow is receive.

questions.question: The politicians have promised to deal with the problem of ...... among young people.
questions.explanation: Unemployment is the problem of being without work. The other options are not problems.

questions.question: A lot of people go to see the movie, it ... be exciting.
questions.explanation: Must expresses strong logical deduction. Many people go, so it must be exciting.

questions.question: We don't think we can ... to see our professor again until next semester.
questions.explanation: Wait to see means have to delay until. 'Can't wait to see' would mean eager, but here it's negative. The intended is 'wait'.

questions.question: I'm sorry for spilling the juice over your carpet. Response:
questions.explanation: Appropriate response to an apology: 'No problem!' or 'That's all right.' Option B is correct.

questions.question: The movie was very sad and she wept. If it ... sad, she ...
questions.explanation: Third conditional: If it hadn't been sad, she wouldn't have wept.

questions.question: Think before you write the answer. ... you may make a mistake.
questions.explanation: Otherwise means if you don't think, then you may make a mistake. 'Otherwise' is correct.

questions.question: It's nearly three years since I last ... to Japan.
questions.explanation: Since + past simple: 'since I last went to Japan'.

questions.question: Steven has never had any accident. He is a ... driver.
questions.explanation: Adjective 'careful' describes the driver. No accidents implies careful.

questions.question: The number of students entering university ... over the last years.
questions.explanation: Risen is intransitive (increased). 'The number' is singular, so 'has risen'. 'Raised' is transitive.

questions.question: I was much ... by the documentary films.
questions.explanation: Past participle as adjective: 'I was much interested' (feeling).

questions.question: A manager of an international company is often given a big ...
questions.explanation: Salary is fixed annual pay for professional/managerial work. Wage is hourly/daily. Salary is correct for a manager.

questions.question: Next week the farmer will be ploughing the field. Passive:
options: Next week the field will be being ploughed by the farmer.
Next week the field will be ploughed by the farmer.
Next week the field will be ploughing by the farmer.
questions.explanation: Future passive: 'will be ploughed'. 'Will be being ploughed' is too awkward and not needed.

questions.question: She put the letters into the wrong envelopes ... mistake.
questions.explanation: By mistake is the correct phrase.

questions.question: The architect's latest design is stunning: , it has been criticized for being impractical to build.
questions.explanation: Contrast between stunning and criticized requires 'however'.

questions.question: Identify the correctly revised version of the run-on sentence: 'The project deadline is tomorrow the team is working late tonight.'
options: The project deadline is tomorrow, the team is working late tonight.
The project deadline is tomorrow; the team is working late tonight.
The project deadline is tomorrow because the team is working late tonight.
The project deadline is tomorrow, so the team is working late tonight.
questions.explanation: A semicolon correctly separates two independent clauses. Option A is a comma splice. Option C changes meaning. Option D adds 'so' which is fine but not just fixing run-on; B is simplest correction.

questions.question: Choose the option that is NOT a run-on sentence:
options: Data analysis is crucial it informs our strategy.
Data analysis is crucial, it informs our strategy.
Data analysis is crucial; it informs our strategy.
Data analysis is crucial, and it informs our strategy.
questions.explanation: Option C uses a semicolon correctly. Option A is run-on, B is comma splice, D is correct as well but has a comma plus conjunction. Both C and D are correct, but the question asks for NOT a run-on. Both C and D are fine. However D uses a comma with 'and' which is also correct. Option C is a single correct answer. Typically, C is the answer.

questions.question: The company can't afford any more delays; we risk ... our biggest client.
questions.explanation: Risk + gerund: 'risk losing'. 'Afford to have' is infinitive. So 'afford to have / risk losing' gives 'to have / losing' which is option A.

questions.question: I distinctly remember him ... the documents yesterday, but he claims ... nothing.
questions.explanation: Remember + gerund (past action) — 'remember giving'. Claims + gerund or infinitive? 'Claims receiving' is fine. Option D 'giving / receiving' both gerunds.

questions.question: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?
options: The ingredients for the recipe are: flour, sugar, eggs, and butter.
The ingredients for the recipe are flour, sugar, eggs, and butter.
The ingredients for the recipe are; flour, sugar, eggs, and butter.
The ingredients for the recipe are - flour, sugar, eggs, and butter.
questions.explanation: No colon is needed after 'are' because it is not a complete sentence introducing a list. Option B is correct without any punctuation before the list.

questions.question: Choose the sentence with correct apostrophe use.
options: The dogs bowl is empty, and its collar is
The dog's bowl is empty, and it's collar is missing.
The dogs' bowl is empty, and its collar is
The dog's bowl is empty, and its collar is missing.
questions.explanation: The dog's bowl (singular possessive), its (possessive pronoun, no apostrophe) collar is missing. Option D is correct.

questions.question: When I was in Paris, I _ a lovely little bakery where they _ the most incredible croissants I had ever tasted.
questions.explanation: Past simple 'found' and 'made' for completed past actions.

questions.question: Shakespeare _ numerous plays and sonnets that _ influential for centuries.
questions.explanation: Shakespeare wrote (past simple) and they have been influential (present perfect, since they still are).

questions.question: By the time the rescue team arrived at the summit, the climbers _ all their supplies and _ for over twelve hours.
questions.explanation: Past perfect 'had exhausted' for earlier action, past perfect continuous 'had been waiting' for duration before arrival.

questions.question: She recognized the melody immediately; she _ it many times in her childhood.
questions.explanation: Past perfect 'had heard' because the hearing happened before the recognition.

questions.question: This is one of the most challenging tasks I _ in my entire career.
questions.explanation: Present perfect 'have ever faced' for experience up to now.

questions.question: The company's stock price _ significantly since the new CEO _ over last January.
questions.explanation: Since + point in past requires present perfect for the main clause: 'has risen'. The second clause is simple past 'took over'.

questions.question: The conference brought together several experts _ research on climate change has been groundbreaking.
questions.explanation: Whose shows possession: experts whose research has been groundbreaking.

questions.question: The proposal, _ was drafted by the legal team, requires the board's final approval.
questions.explanation: Non-restrictive relative clause referring to a thing uses 'which'. 'That' is not used with commas.

questions.question: _ classical music is often structured and complex, jazz celebrates improvisation and spontaneity.
questions.explanation: Whereas introduces a contrast between classical and jazz.

questions.question: The committee _ every second Tuesday to discuss budgetary concerns, and their next meeting _ on the 14th.
questions.explanation: Committee is singular: 'meets'. Present simple for regular schedule, 'is' for future scheduled event (is on the 14th).

questions.question: In this region, the finest silks _ by skilled artisans using techniques that _ for generations.
questions.explanation: Present simple passive 'are produced' for general fact. Present perfect passive 'have been preserved' for techniques passed down.

questions.question: The new policy on remote work _ enthusiastically by the staff, who feel a greater sense of autonomy.
questions.explanation: Passive: policy is welcomed by staff. 'Is welcomed' is correct.

questions.question: I promise I _ you the moment I _ any news from the headquarters.
questions.explanation: Future main clause 'will call', present simple in time clause 'get'.

questions.question: Look at those dark clouds. I think it _; we _ the picnic.
options: is going to rain / should cancel
is raining / are going to cancel
questions.explanation: Evidence-based prediction: 'is going to rain'. Suggestion: 'should cancel'.

questions.question: _ the initial data seemed promising, the researchers decided to repeat the experiment to confirm the results.
questions.explanation: Because gives reason for repeating experiment. The data seemed promising, so they repeated to confirm. 'Because' fits.

questions.question: _ he managed to deliver the project on time and exceed all expectations.
options: As he was under immense pressure
Since he was under immense pressure
So that he was under immense pressure
questions.explanation: Contrast: despite pressure, he succeeded. 'Despite being under immense pressure' is correct.

questions.question: My holiday was never _. I would use every minute for something enjoyable.
questions.explanation: Holiday was not boring; he used every minute. 'Boring' describes the holiday.

questions.question: I think my mother _ in this university since 2000.
questions.explanation: Since 2000 requires present perfect: 'has taught'.

questions.question: I have searched all over and could it be found. Someone must have thrown it out.
questions.explanation: Nowhere (written as 'no where' in option) means not in any place. 'Could it be found nowhere' is intended meaning.

questions.question: I hope you will visit your family next summer, _?
questions.explanation: Tag for positive statement 'I hope you will visit' is 'won't you?' (question tag softens).

questions.question: The Bank, _ next to the hotel, _ last month for the second time.
questions.explanation: Passive simple past: 'was robbed last month'.

questions.question: The woman _ was injured in the accident is now in hospital.
questions.explanation: Relative pronoun for person as subject: 'who'.

questions.question: It is now generally accepted that in the future robots will take over many of our tasks, especially the more _ type of jobs.
questions.explanation: Repetitive means involving repetition, often boring. 'Repetitive jobs' is correct.

questions.question: He has secured his visa and everything necessary for his travel. They say he _ move to U.S.A.
questions.explanation: Planned future action based on evidence: 'is going to move'.

questions.question: My friend sold his old house _ buy a new one. Unfortunately, his plan didn't work out and now he has no house.
questions.explanation: So as to + infinitive expresses purpose. 'Sold his house so as to buy a new one'.

questions.question: If I had had free time, I. shopping.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: would have gone.

questions.question: If I were you, I. that job.
questions.explanation: Would apply for.

questions.question: His manager was so angry because of . late.
questions.explanation: His being late (gerund phrase).

questions.question: Would you mind showing me ...?
options: how can I go to the post-office
questions.explanation: The way to the post-office.

questions.question: Could you hang a minute? I'll be back.
questions.explanation: Hang on.

questions.question: _ a grade 12 student, you have to read wisely and you will pass the exam.
questions.explanation: As a grade 12 student (given your role) is correct.

questions.question: If she hadn't studied harder, she _ what she wants. Now, she is an engineer.
questions.explanation: Third conditional negative: wouldn't have got.

questions.question: The harder he makes a training, _ he is. He becomes famous for Africa.
questions.explanation: The harder... the more (successful) but incomplete. 'The more he is' is not great. Likely 'the more' is the intended comparative.

questions.question: _ making little effort for his final exam, he marks rather high.
options: In spite of the fact that
questions.explanation: Despite + gerund phrase 'making little effort' is correct.

questions.question: He thinks just like childish due to this his friends named him _.
questions.explanation: Immature means not mature.

questions.question: In spite of unstable political situation, the president _ a contact with civil societies about good governance yesterday.
questions.explanation: Active past: 'made a contact'.

questions.question: They feel happy! We expect they _ everything what they need.
questions.explanation: Future simple 'will get' for prediction.

questions.question: Teshale: I am interested in drama. What are you good? Senait: I am good _ poetry.
questions.explanation: Good at something.

questions.question: 'It's time I helped my mother.' This is equivalent with:
options: I still don't help my mother
I am not helped my mother
questions.explanation: It's time + past subjunctive means the action should be done now but hasn't been done. So 'I still don't help my mother' is the closest meaning.

questions.question: I wish they _ the entrance examination. They studied Bid Bang Theory.
questions.explanation: Wish about a past action that didn't happen? They studied, so they likely passed. Wish they had passed? Actually 'I wish they had passed' means they didn't pass. The sentence says 'They studied' — contradictory. Probably they didn't pass? Without clear context, 'had passed' is a common wish pattern.

questions.question: We usually write new words in my vocabulary book _ we can find them easily.
questions.explanation: So that + clause 'we can find'.

questions.question: You haven't done a home work since September, so you are too _.
questions.explanation: Careless means not doing necessary work.

questions.question: The milk which was in the bottle was sour. This is the same as:
options: Some bottle of the milk was sour.
None bottle of the milk was sour.
All bottle of the milk was sour.
One bottle of milk was sour.
questions.explanation: The original says the milk in the bottle (one bottle). 'One bottle of milk was sour' matches.

questions.question: Waitress: Have you ordered? Guest: Black coffee please. Waitress: _?
questions.explanation: The waitress asks 'Have you ordered?' Guest says 'Black coffee please' implying not ordered. Then waitress might confirm 'Have you?' The tag is odd. Among options, 'Have you?' is a repetition.

questions.question: Sport is _ in many schools, but it is compulsory here.
questions.explanation: Optional means not required, opposite of compulsory.

questions.question: When the time he graduates, he _ marry his classmate.
questions.explanation: Planned future: 'is going to marry'.

questions.question: _ slowly he drives, he has an accident.
questions.explanation: However slowly he drives, he has an accident (no matter how slow).

questions.question: By next year this time, they _ a university. They have studied harder.
questions.explanation: Future perfect 'will have joined' by next year.

questions.question: Sorry to say, she had stopped _ alcohol because she had a headache.
questions.explanation: Stopped drinking (ceased the habit).

questions.question: The young boys are late _ school today. They _ a school bus.
questions.explanation: Late for school. 'Must have missed'? None of these fit perfectly. 'Must have left out' is odd. Probably 'must have missed' but not given. Given the options, A is the only one with 'must have'.

questions.question: His accent is difficult to understand, but a few students _ understand what he wants.
questions.explanation: A few students are able to understand (plural).

questions.question: If any wrong thing comes with this plan, _ responsible.
questions.explanation: Future conditional: 'will you hold responsible?'

questions.question: _ was the road that they were almost late for the school.
questions.explanation: So crowded that... (result clause). 'So crowded was the road that...' inversion.

questions.question: Each of us _ hold up those affected by drought. It is not humanistic to leave them to migrate.
questions.explanation: Must expresses strong obligation.

questions.question: My wallet _ out of my pocket before I sat down. No one help to find it.
questions.explanation: Past perfect 'had fallen' before sitting down.

questions.question: Direct speech: She said, 'Oh no! I missed the exam' Indirect Speech:
options: She exclaimed with sorrow that I had missed the exam.
She exclaimed with sorrow that she missed the exam.
She exclaimed with sorrow that I missed the exam.
She exclaimed with sorrow that she had missed the exam.
questions.explanation: She exclaimed with sorrow that she had missed the exam. (backshift 'missed' → 'had missed', pronoun 'I' → 'she').

questions.question: I can't open my house. I _ my key.
questions.explanation: Present perfect 'have lost' for recent past affecting present.

questions.question: The sky is covered with black clouds. I think it _
questions.explanation: Evidence-based prediction: is going to rain.

questions.question: What they are doing is not relevant _ the instruction in the book.
questions.explanation: Relevant to.

questions.question: Please ask if they haven't mailed the document _ It should be here by Monday.
questions.explanation: Yet in negative questions: 'haven't mailed yet'.

questions.question: There are many occasions in which seat belts save life, yet _ drivers often wear them.
questions.explanation: Few (negative) means not many. 'Few drivers wear them' contrasts with occasions they save life.

questions.question: I'm not superstitious, but you _ something earlier than brought you this bad luck.
questions.explanation: Must have done expresses strong deduction about a past action.

questions.question: We don't know why he suspected us. _ from among us would want his money.
questions.explanation: No one (nobody) would want his money.

questions.question: We had _ tea, but there wasn't _ sugar. What shall we do?
questions.explanation: Some in positive, any in negative.

questions.question: She is genuinely broke, _ we have to contribute some for her. She is our friend.
questions.explanation: So indicates result.

questions.question: The purchasers should be accountable for what happened. _ piece is faulty.
questions.explanation: Every piece (singular each).

questions.question: It seems she wants me to do her a favor. You know, she has never been as nice _ me as she is.
questions.explanation: Nice to me.

questions.question: I haven't seen it, but I could imagine that the Renaissance dam is _ than other dams we have.
questions.explanation: Much bigger is correct comparative intensifier.

questions.question: Not all the seats in the exam hall _ taken yet.
questions.explanation: Seats are taken (present simple passive). 'Are' is correct.

questions.question: Selam: 'Have you tried any of those new ride-hailing apps, like Feres or Ride? I'm curious to know what you think.' Mikias: ______
options: I prefer using the blue-and-white taxis, to be honest.
In my view, it's a double-edged sword.
The result is that traffic has gotten worse.
I wish they would expand to other cities like Dire Dawa.
questions.explanation: Mikias says 'The convenience is fantastic, but the surge pricing...' which indicates a double-edged sword (both good and bad). Option B matches.

questions.question: Davit: 'What's your take on the government's focus on establishing industrial parks to boost manufacturing?' Alem: ______
options: I am of the opinion that the benefits outweigh the drawbacks.
I would rather they invested in agriculture first.
As a result, exports have increased.
If only they had consulted more stakeholders.
questions.explanation: Alem's response 'It has great potential for job creation, though...' Option A gives a balanced opinion.

questions.question: Mother: 'Shall we go to Meskel Square in Addis to see the Demera this year, or should we celebrate at home in Debre Zeit as usual?' Father: ______
options: I think the ceremony in Addis is more impressive.
I'd much rather we stay here.
The reason is the traffic is unbearable.
I hope we can do both someday.
questions.explanation: Father says 'It's less crowded, and we can have a larger feast with all our relatives.' That indicates preference for staying home, so 'I'd much rather we stay here' fits.

questions.question: Hana: 'Should we go to Kaldi's or Tomoca for coffee?' Jemal: ______
options: In my opinion, Kaldi's is more modern.
I wish Tomoca had more seating.
As a result, the aroma is incredible.
questions.explanation: Jemal says 'Their traditional brew is stronger and they roast the beans right there' — likely referring to Tomoca. So 'I prefer Tomoca' is the logical response.

questions.question: Interviewer: 'This project involves coordinating with numerous international and local stakeholders. Can you describe a strategy you would use to manage such complex communication?' Interviewee: ______
options: I wish I had more experience in that area.
I would prefer to delegate that to a dedicated team.
My approach would be to implement a centralized reporting system with weekly sync-ups.
The reason is that stakeholders often have conflicting interests.
questions.explanation: The interviewee gives a specific strategy: 'My approach would be to implement a centralized reporting system...' which answers the question directly.

questions.question: Journalist: 'Ethiopian cuisine is gaining global recognition. What do you believe is its most unique selling point on the world stage?' Chef: ______
options: I think it's the flavor of berbere.
Without a doubt, it is the concept of 'gursha' and the shared platter.
I would rather focus on promoting our vegan fasting dishes.
As a result, more restaurants are opening abroad.
questions.explanation: The chef says 'It's not just about the food; it's a communal experience centered around injera...' So the unique selling point is the communal experience, which matches option B.

questions.question: Customer: 'I noticed the price of shiro has gone up significantly since my last visit.' Owner: 'Yes, unfortunately. ______ Consequently, we've had to adjust our menu prices to maintain quality.'
options: I prefer to use the highest quality ingredients.
The main reason is a nationwide shortage of chickpeas due to the poor harvest.
I hope the prices will stabilize soon.
As a result, our costs have doubled.
questions.explanation: The owner explains the reason for price increase: shortage of chickpeas. Then 'consequently' follows.

questions.question: Guide: 'We will visit the first group of rock-hewn churches this morning.' Tourist: 'Why is it better to go in the morning?' Guide: '______ Therefore, the lighting is perfect for photography, and it's much cooler for walking.'
options: I wish we could avoid the afternoon crowds.
Well, the sun rises directly over the sites.
The result is a more pleasant experience.
questions.explanation: The guide explains why morning is better: sun rises directly over the sites, which gives good lighting. Option B gives that reason.

questions.question: Teshome: 'The city has changed so much. These skyscrapers were all just fields when we were young.' Abebe: 'I know. ______ The pace was slower, and everyone knew their neighbors.'
options: I prefer the way it was.
I sometimes wish we could turn back time.
The reason is that modernization was inevitable.
As a result, the community feeling is gone.
questions.explanation: Abebe expresses nostalgia: 'I sometimes wish we could turn back time.' The description of slower pace and neighbors fits.

questions.question: Graduate: 'I've applied to over twenty jobs in the renewable energy sector but haven't gotten a single interview.' Mentor: 'Don't lose hope. Your profile is strong; you just need the right opportunity. ______'
options: I prefer that you also look into related fields.
The result is a very competitive market.
I wish you the very best of luck.
I'd rather you had done more internships.
questions.explanation: Mentor encourages the graduate: 'I wish you the very best of luck' is a supportive closing.

questions.question: Leta: price rises is still on the go up in this country. Don't you think so? Mulugleta: ______
options: I am afraid, it is not.
What did you buy these days?
Is that still a double digit?
questions.explanation: Mulugleta disagrees politely: 'I am afraid, it is not.'

questions.question: Michael: I have bought this shoe for you. Seid: ______ Michael: Don't mention it.
options: Thank you. I will give soon.
questions.explanation: Seid thanks Michael: 'Many thanks. It's so beautiful.'

questions.question: Tegenu: Shall we lend him some money, please? Partnership: ______
options: How much did they pay him for the painting?
That is silly! He didn't pay back the last one.
He can't be serious. He should be teasing.
A cooker would cost her no less than 100$
questions.explanation: Partnership opposes lending because he didn't pay back last time. 'That is silly! He didn't pay back the last one.'

questions.question: Eskindir: Have you heard that Melaku was free? Kefale: ______
options: What good news! When was that?
Is Melaku a rich man by teaching.
I know. He harshly criticizes the system.
questions.explanation: Kefale reacts positively: 'What good news! When was that?'

questions.question: Wagaw: What he has? I received my laptop back. Reshid: ______
options: He has also taken my laptop.
No! You know he wants it.
You want it for his thesis.
Laptops are very necessary to anything.
questions.explanation: Reshid says he also had his laptop taken: 'He has also taken my laptop.'

questions.question: Ephrem: I won two million birr in a lottery. Zimam: ______
options: Did you also win one last year?
Ephrem! I also bought the ticket.
questions.explanation: Zimam expresses surprise: 'Two million! Are you kidding?'

questions.question: Damitew: The roof is leaking. What shall we do? Shewareded: ______
options: No doubt. This is a very old house.
I know. It will be raining soon.
May be; find someone who can fix it.
I thought it was also leaking last year.
questions.explanation: Shewareded suggests a solution: 'Maybe find someone who can fix it.'

questions.question: Lilu: Do you speak Italian? You: ______
questions.explanation: Direct answer to 'Do you speak Italian?' is 'Just a little.'

questions.question: Customer: ______ Shopkeeper: It is 1500ETB.
questions.explanation: The shopkeeper gives a price, so the customer asked 'How much is this camera?'

questions.question: Baysa: Hello, where do you live? Alemitu: ______
questions.explanation: Where do you live? asks for location. 'Around Arat kilo' gives a location.

questions.question: Yonas: Are you married, Zahara? Zahara: ______
questions.explanation: Direct answer: 'No, I am not.'

questions.question: Secretary: How do you do, Sir? Visitor: ______
options: I am fine. How do you do?
I am fine. How are you?
Very well, how do you do?
questions.explanation: The correct response to 'How do you do?' is the same phrase: 'How do you do?'

questions.question: Ali: ______ Almaz: That sounds reasonable.
options: Did she buy a new purse?
questions.explanation: A suggestion 'Shall we go for coffee?' would elicit 'That sounds reasonable.'

questions.question: Fasil: Why did you leave the party early last night? Rahel: ______
questions.explanation: Reason for leaving early: 'I didn't feel well.'

questions.question: Teacher: We are on page 50, aren't we? Student: ______
questions.explanation: The student confirms the page: 'Yes, exercise 4.' (assuming page 50 corresponds to exercise 4).

questions.question: Hanna: ______ Kedija: Kind of, I suppose.
options: What does your boyfriend do for living?
questions.explanation: The response 'Kind of, I suppose' suggests a yes/no question about feelings. 'Do you love your friend?' fits.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the passage? (Sustainable Development in Ethiopia)
questions.passage:
Sustainable Development in the Ethiopian Context

Ethiopia, a nation renowned for its ancient history and cultural heritage, stands at a pivotal crossroads in the 21st century. The pursuit of sustainable development here is a complex but essential endeavor, uniquely shaped by its environmental challenges, economic ambitions, and rich social tapestry. The nation's growth is predominantly agrarian, with a significant portion of its population relying on rain-fed agriculture. This makes it highly vulnerable to the impacts of climate change, such as erratic rainfall patterns and land degradation, which threaten both food security and economic stability. In response, Ethiopia has embarked on an ambitious green development path. The cornerstone of this strategy is the Climate-Resilient Green Economy (CRGE) strategy, launched in 2011. Its primary goal is to achieve middle-income status by 2025 while developing a green economy. This involves a four-pronged approach: protecting and reforesting lands, expanding renewable energy production, adopting climate-resilient agricultural practices, and advancing green technology in industries. Massive investments in hydroelectric, wind, and solar power, like the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam (GERD), aim not only to provide domestic energy but also to position Ethiopia as a regional power exporter.

However, the path is fraught with challenges. Rapid population growth exerts immense pressure on natural resources. Balancing immediate economic needs, such as employment creation and poverty reduction, with long-term environmental goals remains a delicate act. Furthermore, ensuring equitable development across diverse regions and ethnic groups is crucial for social sustainability. The success of these initiatives hinges on effective governance, community involvement, and continued international support. For Ethiopia, sustainable development is not merely an option; it is an imperative for ensuring the well-being of its present and future generations, preserving its environment, and maintaining its unique cultural identity.
options: To detail the ancient history of Ethiopia.
To explain the challenges and strategies of sustainable development in Ethiopia.
To argue for international aid to Ethiopia.
To describe the tourist attractions of Ethiopia.
questions.explanation: The passage explains Ethiopia's sustainable development strategy (CRGE) and the challenges it faces. Main purpose is to explain those.

questions.question: According to the passage, why is Ethiopia's economy particularly vulnerable to climate change?
options: Because it relies heavily on rain-fed agriculture.
Because its population is too small.
questions.explanation: The passage says the nation's growth is predominantly agrarian with rain-fed agriculture, making it vulnerable to climate change.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of Ethiopia's Climate-Resilient Green Economy (CRGE) strategy?
options: To become the world's largest exporter of coffee.
To achieve middle-income status by 2025 with a green economy.
To reduce its population growth rate.
To build the largest dam in Africa.
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Its primary goal is to achieve middle-income status by 2025 while developing a green economy.'

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a part of the CRGE's four-pronged approach?
questions.explanation: The four approaches are: protecting and reforesting, expanding renewable energy, adopting climate-resilient agriculture, advancing green technology. Building larger cities is not mentioned.

questions.question: What is mentioned as a major challenge to sustainable development in Ethiopia?
options: A lack of ancient cultural heritage.
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Rapid population growth exerts immense pressure on natural resources.'

questions.question: In the context of the passage, what does 'pivotal crossroads' (Paragraph 1) most likely mean?
questions.explanation: Pivotal crossroads metaphorically means a critical point where important decisions must be made.

questions.question: The word 'erratic' in the phrase 'erratic rainfall patterns' (Paragraph 1) means:
questions.explanation: Erratic means unpredictable and irregular.

questions.question: What is the best meaning of 'cornerstone' as used in 'The cornerstone of this strategy...' (Paragraph 2)?
questions.explanation: Cornerstone means the most important foundation or basis.

questions.question: The word 'equitable' in 'ensuring equitable development' (Paragraph 3) means:
questions.explanation: Equitable means fair and just.

questions.question: What does 'fraught with' in the phrase 'the path is fraught with challenges' (Paragraph 3) imply?
options: The path is free from challenges.
The path is full of challenges.
The path is made easier by challenges.
questions.explanation: Fraught with means full of (something undesirable).

questions.question: In the first paragraph, what does the pronoun 'This' refer to in the sentence: 'This makes it highly vulnerable...'?
options: The nation's growth being predominantly agrarian.
questions.explanation: This refers to the preceding fact: the nation's growth is predominantly agrarian with rain-fed agriculture.

questions.question: In the second paragraph, what does the pronoun 'Its' refer to in the sentence: 'Its primary goal is to achieve...'?
options: The Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam's (GERD) goal.
The Climate-Resilient Green Economy (CRGE) strategy's goal.
questions.explanation: Its refers to the CRGE strategy.

questions.question: In the second paragraph, what does the pronoun 'This' refer to in the sentence: 'This involves a four-pronged approach...'?
options: The goal of the CRGE strategy.
questions.explanation: This refers to the goal of the CRGE strategy (achieving middle-income status while developing a green economy).

questions.question: In the third paragraph, what does the pronoun 'Its' refer to in the phrase: 'preserving its environment'?
questions.explanation: Its refers to Ethiopia's environment.

questions.question: In the final sentence, what do the pronouns 'its' and 'it' refer to? 'For Ethiopia, sustainable development is not merely an option; it is an imperative for ensuring the well-being of its present and future generations...'
options: its = the present generation's; it = the well-being
its = the option's; it = an imperative
questions.explanation: Its refers to Ethiopia's (generations), it refers to sustainable development.

questions.question: Could you ... the car? There's a large puddle near my door.
questions.explanation: Back out means to reverse out of a space. To avoid a puddle, you'd back out the car.

questions.question: The bus ... so I had to go on foot.
questions.explanation: Broke down means stopped functioning (vehicle).

questions.question: She ... to her kids and parents.
questions.explanation: Gave up her day means sacrificed her day. 'Gave the day up' is possible.

questions.question: Oh, don't pay attention to him, ... your conversation, please.
questions.explanation: Carry on means continue.

questions.question: We had a quarrel last night but we could ... in the morning.
questions.explanation: Make up means reconcile after a quarrel.

questions.question: I'm ill and feel very weak. I have to ...
questions.explanation: Call off means cancel.

questions.question: I've ... three T-shirts. Which one suits me better?
questions.explanation: Picked out means selected.

questions.question: I'm not a person who can ... teenagers.
questions.explanation: Put up with means tolerate.

questions.question: I hate job interviews because it always seems like they ... me.
questions.explanation: Look down on means regard with contempt.

questions.question: Our project ... very fine.
questions.explanation: Works out means proceeds or develops.

questions.question: She always ... when she wears a new dress.
questions.explanation: Shows off means displays proudly.

questions.question: ... icicles on the roof.
questions.explanation: Watch out for means be careful of (icicles).

questions.question: ... and tell me what's happened?
questions.explanation: Quiet down means become quiet; context suggests asking to calm and talk.

questions.question: ... if you visit Madrid! I'm always glad to see you.
questions.explanation: Drop in means visit informally.

questions.question: She ... Matt's name ... when she made a wedding guest list.
questions.explanation: Left out means omitted.

questions.question: Hey! Quit ... and help me repair the gate!
questions.explanation: Goofing off means wasting time.

questions.question: We have already been discussing the problem for 2 hours. I think it's time to ... our conference.
questions.explanation: Wrap up means conclude.

questions.question: My manager wants me ... the report ...
questions.explanation: Do over means redo.

questions.question: I ... my Saturday walk in the park because of the oncoming frost.
questions.explanation: Called off means canceled.

questions.question: The fire ... in a two-storey building which we had been living in before.
questions.explanation: Broke out (of fire) means started suddenly.

questions.question: Steven ... He failed to keep his word.
questions.explanation: Let me down means disappointed or failed to keep a promise.

questions.question: He was so loud and noisy in the morning that I had to get up and ...
questions.explanation: Chew him out means scold him angrily.

questions.question: The film was so tedious that she ... during it.
questions.explanation: Nodded off means fell asleep.

questions.question: She always supports me. She ... over my decision to move to London.
questions.explanation: Backed me up means supported me.

questions.question: He ... but nobody told him how to get to the park.
questions.explanation: Asked around means asked many people for information.

questions.question: The company ... the internet in our hotel.
questions.explanation: Cut off means disconnected.

questions.question: We had been friends for many years but we have finally ... We entered different colleges.
questions.explanation: Grown apart means become distant.

questions.question: Steve is always ... stories about why he comes home late!
questions.explanation: Making up means inventing (false stories).

questions.question: Could you ... the test papers ... before the students?
questions.explanation: Pass out means distribute.

questions.question: My brother ... our grandfather. They both are stubborn and never give up.
questions.explanation: Takes after means resembles in character.

questions.question: After the scandal, the CEO tried to ... the rumors by holding a press conference, but few believed his explanations.
questions.explanation: Put to rest means stop or eliminate (rumors).

questions.question: She's very good at ... complex ideas in a way that even beginners can understand.
questions.explanation: Putting across means communicating effectively.

questions.question: We need to ... some money every month for our daughter's future education.
questions.explanation: Put aside means save (money).

questions.question: As a historian, she spends her days ... information from ancient manuscripts.
questions.explanation: Looking for means searching for.

questions.question: He really ... his uncle, who was a self-made and respected entrepreneur.
questions.explanation: Looks up to means admires and respects.

questions.question: Don't ... his grumpy demeanor; he's actually one of the kindest people you'll ever meet.
questions.explanation: Look down on means judge as inferior. The sentence advises not to be misled by his grumpiness into looking down on him.

questions.question: It took me weeks to ... the flu I caught on holiday.
questions.explanation: Get over means recover from (illness).

questions.question: He managed to ... not doing any work for months by blaming IT issues.
questions.explanation: Get away with means avoid punishment for.

questions.question: It was a tough year, but we managed to ... it by supporting each other.
questions.explanation: Get through means survive or endure a difficult period.

questions.question: The striking workers won't ... until their demands for a pay rise are met.
questions.explanation: Back down means yield or retreat from a position.

questions.question: I promised I would help him move, and I can't ... now at the last minute.
questions.explanation: Back out means withdraw from a commitment.

questions.question: Can you ... your argument with some solid data? I'm not convinced yet.
questions.explanation: Back up means support with evidence.

questions.question: According to the passage, what may be the most dangerous aspect of Type II diabetes? (Diabetes passage, question 229 in original numbering)
questions.passage:
There are two types of diabetes, insulin-dependent and non-insulin dependent. Between 90-95% of the estimated 13-14 million people in the United States with diabetes have non insulin dependent, or Type II, diabetes. Because this form of diabetes usually begins in adults over the age of 40 and is most common after the age of 55, it used to be called adult-onset diabetes. Its symptoms often develop gradually and are hard to identify at first; therefore, nearly half of all people with diabetes do not know they have it. For instance, someone who has developed Type II diabetes may feel tired or ill without knowing why. This can be particularly dangerous because untreated diabetes can cause damage to the heart, blood vessels, eyes, kidneys, and nerves. While the causes, short-term effects, and treatments of the two types of diabetes differ, both types can cause the same long-term health problems.

Most importantly, both types affect the body's ability to use digested food for energy. Diabetes does not interfere with digestion, but it does prevent the body from using an important product of digestion, glucose (commonly known as sugar), for energy. After a meal, the normal digestive system breaks some food down into glucose. The blood carries the glucose or sugar throughout the body, causing blood glucose levels to rise. In response to this rise, the hormone insulin is released into the bloodstream and signals the body tissues to metabolize or burn the glucose for fuel, which causes blood glucose levels to return to normal. The glucose that the body does not use right away is stored in the liver, muscle, or fat.

In both types of diabetes, however, this normal process malfunctions. A gland called the pancreas, found just behind the stomach, makes insulin. In people with insulin-dependent diabetes, the pancreas does not produce insulin at all. This condition usually begins in childhood and is known as Type I (formerly called juvenile-onset) diabetes. These patients must have daily insulin injections to survive. People with non-insulin-dependent diabetes usually produce some insulin in their pancreas, but their bodies' tissues do not respond well to the insulin signal and, therefore, do not metabolize the glucose properly, a condition known as insulin resistance. Insulin resistance is an important factor in non-insulin dependent diabetes, and scientists are searching for the causes of insulin resistance. They have identified two possibilities. The first is that there could be a defect in the insulin receptors on cells. Like an appliance that needs to be plugged into an electrical outlet, insulin has to bind to a receptor in order to function. Several things can go wrong with receptors. For example, there may not be enough receptors to which insulin may bind, or a defect in the receptors may prevent insulin from binding. The second possible cause of insulin resistance is that, although insulin may bind to the receptors, the cells do not read the signal to metabolize the glucose. Scientists continue to study these cells to see why this might happen.

There's no cure for diabetes yet. However, there are ways to alleviate its symptoms. In 1986, a National Institute of Health panel of experts recommended that the best treatment for non-insulin dependent diabetes is a diet that helps one maintain a normal weight and pays particular attention to a proper balance of the different food groups. Many experts, including those in the American Diabetes Association, recommend that 50-60% of daily calories come from carbohydrates, 12-20% from protein, and no more than 30% from fat. Foods that are rich in carbohydrates, like breads, cereals, fruits, and vegetables, break down into glucose during digestion, causing blood glucose to rise. Additionally, studies have shown that cooked foods raise blood glucose higher than raw, unpeeled foods. A doctor or nutritionist should always be consulted for more of this kind of information and for help in planning a diet to offset the effects of this form of diabetes.
options: Insulin shots are needed daily for treatment of Type II diabetes.
Type II diabetes may go undetected and, therefore, untreated.
In Type II diabetes, the pancreas does not produce insulin.
Type II diabetes interferes with digestion.
questions.explanation: The passage says symptoms develop gradually and are hard to identify, so nearly half do not know they have it. Untreated diabetes can cause damage, making this the most dangerous aspect.

questions.question: Which of the following are the same for Type I and Type II diabetes?
questions.explanation: The passage says: 'both types can cause the same long-term health problems' (long-term health risks).

questions.question: Which of the following is the main function of insulin?
options: It signals tissues to metabolize sugar.
It breaks down food into glucose.
It carries glucose throughout the body.
questions.explanation: The passage: 'insulin is released... and signals the body tissues to metabolize or burn the glucose for fuel.'

questions.question: Which of the following statements best summarizes the main theme of the passage?
options: Type I and Type II diabetes are best treated by maintaining a high-protein diet.
Type II diabetes is a distinct condition that can be managed by maintaining a healthy diet.
Type I diabetes is an insidious condition most harmful when the patient is not taking daily insulin injections.
Adults who suspect they may have Type II diabetes should immediately adopt a high carbohydrate diet.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on Type II diabetes, its causes, and its management through diet. Option B captures that.

questions.question: Which of the following is mentioned in the passage as a possible problem with insulin receptors in insulin-resistant individuals?
options: Overeating causes the receptors to function improperly.
There may be an overabundance of receptors present.
A defect causes the receptors to bind with glucose.
A defect hinders the receptors from binding with insulin.
questions.explanation: The passage says 'a defect in the receptors may prevent insulin from binding.'

questions.question: 'They' paragraph four line two refers to
questions.passage: same passage (paragraph 4: 'They have identified two possibilities.')
questions.explanation: Scientists are the subject of the previous sentences; they have identified possibilities.

questions.question: According to the passage, in normal individuals, which of the following processes occur immediately after the digestive system converts some food into glucose?
options: The glucose is metabolized by body tissues.
Insulin is released into the bloodstream.
The pancreas manufactures increased amounts of insulin.
questions.explanation: The passage: 'The blood carries the glucose... causing blood glucose levels to rise. In response to this rise, the hormone insulin is released...' So the immediate result is blood sugar levels rise.

questions.question: Based on the information in the passage, which of the following best describes people with Type I diabetes?
options: They do not need to be treated with injections of insulin.
They comprise the majority of people with diabetes.
Their pancreases do not produce insulin.
They are usually diagnosed as adults.
questions.explanation: The passage: 'In people with insulin-dependent diabetes, the pancreas does not produce insulin at all.' That is Type I.

questions.question: What is the closest meaning of the underlined word 'offset' in the final sentence of the passage?
questions.explanation: Offset means to counteract or compensate for the effects of.

questions.question: 'I regret to inform you that we are unable to offer you a post at this time.' This is mostly taken from a letter of
questions.explanation: This is a standard rejection letter for a job application.

questions.question: 'Love is a very powerful force; it conquers barriers of color, race, religion, and culture. It thrives on mutual nurturing of each other,' it is very likely that the writing is:
questions.explanation: The passage makes a claim about love's power, which is argumentative (persuasive).

questions.question: What would be an appropriate ending for Yohannes, a graduate, who is writing a job application letter?
questions.explanation: In a formal job application letter, 'Yours sincerely' or 'Sincerely' are common. Among options, 'With best regards' is acceptable. 'Faithfully yours' is for when name is unknown. 'Truly yours' is also possible, but 'Best regards' is fine.

questions.question: Of the following sentences which is correctly punctuated?
options: Unpredictably, Dr. Belete, academic dean, college of science is so much assertive.
Unpredictably Dr. Belete, academic dean, college of science, is so much assertive.
Unpredictably, Dr. Belete, Academic dean college of science, is so much assertive.
Unpredictably, Dr. Belete, Academic Dean, College of Science, is so much assertive.
questions.explanation: Option D uses commas correctly around the appositive 'Academic Dean, College of Science' and capitalizes titles properly.

questions.question: 'Considering the pressure of today's society on marriage and family, remaining single is a difficult and courageous decision, but one that could ensure happiness and contentment throughout life.' This piece of writing is most likely:
questions.explanation: The sentence presents an opinion and argues for a position, so argumentative.

questions.question: 'I have worked in a hospital for many years. The hospital is called Tikur Anbassa; a big combined hospital which is located in the capital of Ethiopia and provides every kind of services to residents 24 hours a day. This is the best hospital that I have ever been a part of. I am very glad I have worked in this good environment.' which piece of writing will be best conclusion for the above essay?
options: The co-workers will keep helping patients until they recover.
This is a good hospital for everyone who needs to go there.
I devote my knowledge to all of the patients.
All workers are very kind to help patients.
questions.explanation: The paragraph describes the hospital as good; a logical conclusion is that it's a good hospital for everyone.

questions.question: Which one of the following piece of writing is a type of argumentative essay?
options: Animals should not be used for laboratory tests.
Meanwhile, I packed up my little hospital until another day.
Computers can be used for specialized fields as well.
Bahir Dar has a beautiful, big and colorful lake called Tana.
questions.explanation: Option A expresses a position and implies argumentation. The others are statements of fact or narrative.

questions.question: 'As we can see from the above, farmers are more important than doctors for Ethiopian sovereignty.' This is most likely taken from the section of an essay.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'As we can see from the above' signals a concluding sentence.

questions.question: 'Yesterday evening I got home from school around four o'clock. My mother had dinner prepared which we eat as soon as my elders came home from work.' This piece of writing most likely taken from a type of essay.
questions.explanation: It tells a sequence of events in the past, so narrative.

questions.question: 'Thank you for your consideration!' This is for
questions.explanation: Thank you for your consideration is typically used in formal contexts like after a meeting or job application.

questions.question: Which one of the following IS a type of descriptive essay?
options: His dirty bike was plastered with wet and sticky mud.
Non-organic gardeners avoid broad spectrum pesticides as possible.
Laws should restrict where people can smoke as research proves that only harm.
A terrible storm blew up to fifty miles an hour and it rained very hard last night.
questions.explanation: Option A describes the appearance of a bike using sensory details, so descriptive. Option D is also descriptive but more narrative. A is more purely descriptive.

questions.question: 'Here, the potter kneads the clay to prepare it for throwing. Wedging can't be rushed. A minimum of fifty turns is required to rid the clay of air pockets and align its molecules. Once wedged and formed into a ball, the clay is thrown on to the center of a potter's wheel. The material must be centered. Centering not only takes strength but time as well.' Which one is the best title for the above entitled discussion?
options: The process of exchanging material of pottery.
questions.explanation: The passage describes the steps of making pottery, so 'The process of making pottery' is appropriate.

questions.question: Which one of the following is wrongly spelt?
questions.explanation: All are correctly spelled. But 'Boutique' is often misspelled? Actually it's correct. Maybe 'Receipt' has a silent p, still correct. All correct. Possibly 'Restaurant' is correct. The question might have a trick. Typically 'Boutique' is correct. I'll assume none are wrong, but if forced, 'Boutique' is the least common.

questions.question: 'Buckle your seat belt!' This is a warning for
questions.explanation: Seat belt warning is for transport (cars, planes).

questions.question: Which one of the following sentences has a correct punctuation?
options: He said, "Every term has its meaning"; however, we didn't know what it meant.
He said, "Every term has its meaning;" however, we didn't know what it meant.
He said, "every term has its meaning;" however, we didn't know what it meant.
He said "Every term has its meaning." however, we didn't know what it meant.
questions.explanation: Option A uses semicolon correctly outside the closing quote, and 'however' is correctly punctuated. The quote capital 'Every' is correct.

questions.question: Which one of the following sentences is most likely taken from descriptive writing?
options: We were walking around the apartment.
There were people who cleaned the apartment daily.
The apartment was so attractive when seen from distance.
They were renovating the apartment when we had our meeting.
questions.explanation: Option C describes the attractiveness of the apartment, using an adjective and sensory impression.

questions.question: 'Mr. Wolf went out hunting in the forest one dark murky night.' This article is most likely to be a/an text.
questions.explanation: This is the beginning of a story, so narrative.

questions.question: Which one of the following words is correctly spelt?
questions.explanation: 'Receipt' is correctly spelled. 'Recieve' should be receive, 'inquiry' is correct? Actually 'inquiry' is correct. 'Arange' should be arrange. So both B and C are correct? The test likely expects B.
